section.title: Preparations to Build the Temple
section.sectionRationale: Before a single stone of the temple is laid, Solomon must gather materials and skill he does not have at home. He conscripts a workforce, writes to Hiram king of Tyre for cedar, craftsmen, and provisions, and Hiram replies with a blessing and the promised help. The chapter is entirely preparation, yet it already confesses the theology that will govern the whole project: the house is not a container for God but a place to burn sacrifice before a God too great for heaven itself to hold.
section.thesis: Solomon's careful, humble preparations, admitting that no house could ever contain the God of heaven, show that the temple was conceived from the outset not as a cage for deity but as a place of sacrifice and prayer, built with willing help even from a foreign king who blesses the God of Israel.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Solomon's purpose and workforce, verses 1 to 2; his message to Hiram confessing God's greatness and requesting materials, verses 3 to 10; and Hiram's reply, provision, and the numbering of the workforce, verses 11 to 18.

units.label: Solomon's purpose and workforce
units.note: a house for the Name of the LORD

verses.text: Now Solomon purposed to build a {{bayit|house}} for the {{shem|Name}} of the LORD and a royal palace for himself.
So he conscripted 70,000 porters, 80,000 stonecutters in the mountains, and 3,600 supervisors.

units.label: Solomon's message to Hiram
units.note: who can build Him a house

verses.text: Then Solomon sent word to Hiram king of Tyre: "Do for me as you did for my father David when you sent him cedars to build himself a house to live in.

Behold, I am about to build a house for the Name of the LORD my God to dedicate to Him for burning fragrant incense before Him, for displaying the showbread continuously, and for making burnt offerings every morning and evening as well as on the Sabbaths, New Moons, and appointed feasts of the LORD our God. This is ordained for Israel forever.
The house that I am building will be great, for our God is greater than all gods.

But who is able to build a house for Him, since the {{shamayim|heavens}}, even the highest heavens, cannot contain Him? Who then am I, that I should build a house for Him, except as a place to burn sacrifices before Him?

Send me, therefore, a craftsman skilled in engraving to work with gold and silver, with bronze and iron, and with purple, crimson, and blue yarn. He will work with my craftsmen in Judah and Jerusalem, whom my father David provided.
Send me also cedar, cypress, and algum logs from Lebanon, for I know that your servants have skill to cut timber there. And indeed, my servants will work with yours
to prepare for me timber in abundance, because the temple I am building will be great and wonderful.
I will pay your servants, the woodcutters, 20,000 cors of ground wheat, 20,000 cors of barley, 20,000 baths of wine, and 20,000 baths of olive oil."

units.label: Hiram's reply and the workforce numbered
units.note: a skillful man, endowed with understanding

verses.text: Then Hiram king of Tyre wrote a letter in reply to Solomon: "Because the LORD loves His people, He has set you over them as king."

And Hiram added: "Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, who made the heavens and the earth! He has given King David a {{chokmah|wise}} son with insight and understanding, who will build a temple for the LORD and a royal palace for himself.
So now I am sending you Huram-abi, a skillful man endowed with creativity.

He is the son of a woman from the daughters of Dan, and his father is a man of Tyre. He is skilled in work with gold and silver, bronze and iron, stone and wood, purple, blue, and crimson yarn, and fine linen. He is experienced in every kind of engraving and can execute any design that is given him. He will work with your craftsmen and with those of my lord, your father David.
Now let my lord send to his servants the wheat, barley, olive oil, and wine he promised.
We will cut logs from Lebanon, as many as you need, and we will float them to you as rafts by sea down to Joppa. Then you can take them up to Jerusalem."
Solomon numbered all the foreign men in the land of Israel following the census his father David had conducted, and there were found to be 153,600 in all.
Solomon made 70,000 of them porters, 80,000 stonecutters in the mountains, and 3,600 supervisors.

shem.gloss: Name. Solomon builds not a house for God to be confined in, but a house for God's shem, His revealed character and presence, to dwell there, repeated throughout chapters 2 and 6 as the theological key to what the temple is and is not.

bayit.gloss: House. The word runs through the chapter for both the temple, God's house, and Solomon's own royal palace, deliberately paired so that the reader notices Solomon is building two houses, and only one of them could ever be great enough, and even that one cannot contain its true occupant.

chokmah.gloss: Wisdom, skill. Hiram praises David's son as a wise son with insight and understanding, and sends a craftsman, Huram-abi, endowed with the same kind of skilled understanding to work with gold, silver, bronze, and every craft, showing that the wisdom God gave Solomon in chapter 1 now extends even to Israel's foreign partners recognizing and serving it.

shamayim.gloss: Heaven, heavens. Before a single stone is cut, Solomon confesses that the shamayim, even the highest heavens, cannot contain God, so the temple he is about to build can never be more than a place to burn sacrifice before Him, a truth he will repeat and expand in his dedicatory prayer, chapter 6.

basic.context: Before construction begins, Solomon organizes his workforce and writes to Hiram, the king of Tyre who had supplied his father David with cedar. Solomon's letter is remarkable for what it confesses up front, our God is greater than all gods, and the heavens themselves cannot contain Him, so this house can only ever be a place to offer sacrifice, never a home that holds God captive. Hiram replies with genuine praise for the God of Israel and sends timber, provisions, and a master craftsman, Huram-abi, to help build it.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative describing a specific building project and diplomatic exchange; it is not a manual for construction projects today. What is enduring is the theology Solomon states before he builds anything: no structure, however great, can contain the infinite God, and even genuine worship spaces exist only as places to meet with Him, not to house Him.
basic.originalLanguages: Solomon writes of building a bayit, a house, for the shem, the Name, of the LORD, language that frames the whole project around God's revealed presence rather than His physical containment. He confesses that even the shamayim, the highest heavens, cannot hold God, and Hiram in turn recognizes the chokmah, wisdom, God has given Solomon, sending a craftsman of matching skill to help build the house.
basic.typology: The temple built to house God's Name yet unable to contain Him anticipates the incarnation, when the Word who filled heaven and earth was pleased to dwell in a human body, and later the church, described as a spiritual house or temple built of living stones. Huram-abi, a skilled craftsman from a mixed Israelite and Tyrian household, foreshadows the gathering of the nations into the service and worship of the true temple, Christ and His people.
basic.christ: Solomon confesses that no house, however great, can contain the God of heaven, a truth answered fully when the eternal Word became flesh and dwelt among us, John 1:14, the true temple in whom the fullness of deity dwells bodily, Colossians 2:9. Where Solomon needed a foreign king's help to gather cedar and craftsmen, Christ Himself is both the builder and the cornerstone of a greater house, His church, gathered from every nation, 1 Peter 2:4-6.
basic.god: God is so vast that even the highest heavens cannot contain Him, yet He is willing to have His Name dwell among His people in a house built by human hands. He is a God whose greatness even outsiders like Hiram can recognize and praise, and He supplies the skill, materials, and cooperation needed to accomplish what He has purposed, often through unexpected partners.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Kings 5 gives the parallel account of Solomon's correspondence with Hiram. Acts 7:48 to 50 echoes Solomon's own confession that the Most High does not dwell in houses made by human hands. Ephesians 2:19 to 22 describes believers being built together into a dwelling place for God by the Spirit. And 1 Peter 2:4 to 6 calls the church living stones built into a spiritual house.
basic.oneStory: Even before the temple rises, its theology is already settled: God cannot be contained by any house, yet He graciously allows His Name to dwell among His people. Hiram's willing help from outside Israel previews the gathering of the nations into the worship of the true God, a gathering that comes to full flower in Christ, the true temple, and His church, a house built of living stones from every people.

context.literary: The chapter is structured as correspondence framing action: a brief statement of Solomon's intent and workforce, verses 1 to 2, his letter to Hiram which doubles as a confession of faith, verses 3 to 10, and Hiram's reply which doubles as a Gentile king's doxology, verses 11 to 16, closing with the numbering of the workforce that brackets the whole unit, verses 17 to 18 echoing verse 2.
context.historical: Tyre, a Phoenician coastal city, was famous for skilled craftsmen and access to the cedar forests of Lebanon; David had already used Hiram's resources to build his own palace, 2 Samuel 5:11, so Solomon's request continues an established royal relationship. The conscripted labor of foreign residents in the land, distinct from native Israelites, reflects the Chronicler's later note in chapter 8 that these were descendants of the peoples not fully driven out at the conquest.
context.canonical: This chapter parallels 1 Kings 5 but the Chronicler intensifies Solomon's confession of God's transcendence, our God is greater than all gods, and the heavens cannot contain Him, language that will be repeated and expanded in Solomon's dedicatory prayer in chapter 6, forming a deliberate theological bracket around the temple's construction.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the temple's builders themselves, Israelite king and Gentile king alike, understood from the outset that no house could contain God, so that later readers, the returned exiles without a king or a rebuilt temple to match Solomon's, would trust that God's presence was never dependent on the size or grandeur of any building.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical building project and a particular diplomatic exchange; it does not prescribe how believers today must fund or construct places of worship. What is enduring and prescriptive is the theological confession embedded in the narrative, that God transcends every structure and yet graciously consents to dwell among His people.

debates.question: Why would Hiram, a pagan king, praise the LORD as the maker of heaven and earth, verse 12?

views.label: Genuine recognition of Israel's God
views.case: Ancient Near Eastern diplomatic letters often included praise of the recipient's deity as a courtesy; Hiram's words may reflect real, if partial, acknowledgment of the LORD's greatness prompted by Israel's growing reputation under David and Solomon, similar to the queen of Sheba's confession in chapter 9.

views.label: Diplomatic courtesy with theological overtones the Chronicler highlights
views.case: Whatever Hiram's personal convictions, the Chronicler includes his words because they serve his larger purpose, showing that even outsiders recognize the LORD's incomparable greatness, reinforcing the confession Solomon has just made in his own letter.

debates.bearing: Either way, the effect is the same: a foreign king's mouth confirms what Solomon has already confessed, that the God of Israel is greater than all gods, preparing the reader for the queen of Sheba's fuller confession later in the book.

study.originalLanguages: Solomon writes of a bayit, a house, built for the shem, the Name, of the LORD, careful language that locates God's presence in His revealed character rather than confining Him to timber and stone. He confesses that not even the shamayim, the highest heavens, can contain God, and Hiram answers by praising David's son as one given chokmah, wisdom, sending a craftsman of matching skill to build the house that can only ever be a place for sacrifice, never a cage for deity.

typology.type: The house built for God's Name that cannot contain Him
typology.fulfillment: The Word made flesh who tabernacled among us, John 1:14, and the church built together as God's dwelling place by the Spirit, Ephesians 2:22.

typology.type: Huram-abi, a craftsman of mixed Israelite and Tyrian descent skilled to build God's house
typology.fulfillment: The gathering of the nations into the building and worship of the true temple, Christ and His people, 1 Peter 2:4-6.

study.christ: Solomon confesses before he builds anything that the heavens, even the highest heavens, cannot contain God, a truth that finds its full answer when the eternal Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, John 1:14, the true and greater temple in whom the fullness of deity dwells bodily, Colossians 2:9. Where Solomon depended on a foreign king's materials and craftsmen to build a house that would one day be destroyed, Christ Himself builds an everlasting house, His church, as the cornerstone of a spiritual temple gathered from every nation, 1 Peter 2:4-6, Ephesians 2:20-22.
study.god: God is so vast that even the highest heavens cannot contain Him, and yet He graciously consents to let His Name, His revealed presence, dwell among His people in a house built by human hands. His greatness is such that even a pagan king recognizes and praises Him, and He supplies exactly what is needed, skill, materials, and willing partners, to accomplish the work He has purposed.

crossReferences.note: The parallel account of Solomon's correspondence with Hiram and the provision of cedar and craftsmen.
Stephen echoes Solomon's own confession that the Most High does not dwell in houses made by human hands.
Believers are built together into a dwelling place for God by the Spirit, the ultimate house for His Name.
Believers as living stones built into a spiritual house, with Christ as the cornerstone.

study.oneStory: Even before construction begins, the theology of the temple is settled: no house, however great, can contain the God of heaven, yet He graciously allows His Name to dwell among His people. A foreign king's willing help and praise of the LORD anticipates the gathering of every nation into true worship, fulfilled at last in Christ, the true temple, and in His church, a house of living stones built from every people on earth.

quiz.q: What did Solomon confess about God even before construction of the temple began?

options.text: That the heavens, even the highest heavens, cannot contain Him, so the temple could only ever be a place to burn sacrifice before Him.
options.note: Verse 6 records this confession directly, framing the whole project's theology.

options.text: That the temple would be able to fully contain and localize God's presence.
options.note: Solomon explicitly denies this is possible in verse 6.

options.text: That God did not care whether a temple was built at all.
options.note: Solomon clearly regards the temple as important and worth building great and wonderful, verse 9.

options.text: The chapter records no theological reflection at all.
options.note: Verses 5-6 contain explicit theological confession.

quiz.q: Who did Solomon write to for help building the temple, and why?

options.text: Hiram king of Tyre, because he had previously supplied cedar and skilled craftsmen to Solomon's father David.
options.note: Verse 3 explicitly recalls Hiram's earlier help to David as the basis for Solomon's request.

options.text: Pharaoh of Egypt, for gold and silver.
options.note: The chapter's request is to Hiram of Tyre for timber and craftsmen, not to Egypt for precious metals.

options.text: The king of Aram, for horses and chariots.
options.note: This chapter concerns Hiram of Tyre and building materials, not Aram or military equipment.

options.text: No one; Solomon built the temple using only Israelite labor and materials.
options.note: The chapter explicitly describes Solomon's request to Hiram and the arrival of Huram-abi.

quiz.q: How did Hiram respond to Solomon's request?

options.text: He blessed the LORD, praised Solomon's God-given wisdom, and sent timber along with the skilled craftsman Huram-abi.
options.note: Verses 11-16 record Hiram's blessing, praise, and practical provision.

options.text: He refused, saying Tyre owed Israel nothing.
options.note: The opposite occurs; Hiram willingly agrees and sends help.

options.text: He demanded that Solomon convert to the gods of Tyre first.
options.note: No such demand appears; Hiram instead praises the LORD, the God of Israel.

options.text: He sent no reply at all.
options.note: Verses 11-16 record a detailed reply and provision from Hiram.

quiz.q: What does Hiram's praise of the LORD in verse 12 suggest?

options.text: Even a foreign king recognized and praised the greatness of Israel's God and the wisdom He had given Solomon.
options.note: Verse 12 has Hiram blessing the LORD who made heaven and earth and praising David's wise son.

options.text: That Hiram secretly worshiped the LORD as his only god from birth.
options.note: The text records his praise but does not claim this level of prior devotion; it is presented as recognition prompted by what he sees in Solomon and Israel.

options.text: That Hiram mocked Solomon's request.
options.note: Hiram's words are praise and blessing, not mockery.

options.text: The verse contains no reference to God at all.
options.note: Verse 12 explicitly names the LORD, the God of Israel, who made the heavens and the earth.

quiz.q: How does Solomon's confession that heaven cannot contain God point forward in Scripture?

options.text: To the incarnation, when the eternal Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and to the church built as God's dwelling place by the Spirit.
options.note: John 1:14 and Ephesians 2:19-22 both develop this trajectory from a house for God's Name to God dwelling among His people directly.

options.text: To the idea that no temple or place of worship is ever needed.
options.note: Solomon still builds the temple as a genuine place to meet God; the point is that it does not contain Him, not that such places are pointless.

options.text: To a complete rejection of all religious buildings in the New Testament.
options.note: The New Testament reinterprets the temple through Christ and the church rather than simply rejecting sacred space altogether.

options.text: It has no connection to anything in the New Testament.
options.note: Acts 7:48-50 and other texts directly echo this very confession.

questions: Solomon confessed God's greatness before he had built anything. Where in your life do you need to remember God's greatness before tackling a task, rather than after?
Hiram, an outsider, recognized and praised the God of Israel. Who around you might be watching your life and drawing conclusions about God, whether you realize it or not?
Solomon relied on skilled help from beyond Israel to build God's house. Where do you need to humbly accept help from others rather than trying to do everything alone?
The temple could never contain God, yet it was still worth building well. How do you hold together reverence for God's greatness with careful, excellent effort in the tasks He gives you?
Christ is described as a greater temple, and His church as a house of living stones. How does knowing you are part of that living house shape how you see your own part in the church this week?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes that cypress may also be rendered pine, juniper, or fir, and that algum is likely a variant of the almug wood mentioned in 1 Kings 10:11.

The measures given, 20,000 cors of wheat and barley and 20,000 baths of wine and oil, are enormous quantities, roughly thousands of tons and tens of thousands of gallons, underscoring the scale of Solomon's provision for Hiram's workers.
Huram-abi's name is a variant of Hiram; abi may function as an honorific title, master or my father, rather than a strict patronymic, distinguishing the craftsman from the king who shares his name.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Chronicles 2:1-18. No em dashes or en dashes remain; all such marks in the source were converted to commas.
